speaker: interviewer
text: --it's like I knew she can. But we're rolling tape. If you can just ID this tape.

speaker: interviewer
text: What is it, tape five?

speaker: interviewer
text: Yeah. Tape five.

speaker: interviewee
text: So, should I bring something to drink?

speaker: interviewer
text: Sure. Just one second.

speaker: interviewee
text: OK.

speaker: interviewer
text: It's July 7, 1995. We're interviewing Magda Mautner Marx. This is tape five.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. Great. I'm fine.

speaker: interviewer
text: I'm fine, too.

speaker: interviewer
text: David, do you want to--

speaker: interviewer
text: I just want to stand for a minute. I've been sitting for quite a while with my legs crossed. All right.

speaker: interviewer
text: Speed.

speaker: interviewee
text: So then we got this job. And they disappeared into this. Then I felt like I am the provider for this family. So I went again, and I said that I am a nurse, and I want to work. OK.

speaker: interviewer
text: So they took me to an operating room. Were very big fighting still between Buda and Pest-- between the Russian and the German. But not even Pest, but in Buda, because they was holding on on some points. And from here were the fighting. So I went-- they say they need nurses. I thought, OK, I-- in my profession, I know how to make a bandage and this and that. But they took-- put me in the operating room. And they brought in soldiers by the truckload. And they give me-- at first, I had to take off the hair everywhere around the wound with a, with a razor blade.

speaker: interviewee
text: It was terrible what I saw, you know. And then, one day, was an old man-- maybe was 40 years old-- and had a wound above the hip. So he was bandaged and everything in it. He couldn't go to the bathroom. And they gave it to me to wash and to bandage. And then one day, they cut off the leg of a, of a soldier-- amputated.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I had to hold the leg. And the other side was a big Russian. And I was like this paper, so white. And he was laughing at me. So these-- I don't want to, to, to tell in details. But we got food.

speaker: interviewee
text: They gave me a big, big pail of rice and some meat or something in it. I couldn't eat it, because it was with tomato. And for me, this looked like blood. And all day to see this blood, I couldn't eat.

speaker: interviewer
text: So this was. And then one day, we saw that the Russians are coming and going and going and coming in the town. I told my family there, let's go. Let's go that way where the Russian go. It was-- you never saw such a thing like-- you saw at night, you know, the cannon balls and the-- like fireballs. It's beautiful but terrible. So I lost this family. And suddenly I was alone. But I went. I went, I went. And we arrived to a place. And again I saw an empty room. It was a bedroom with a mirror-- you know, this three-side mirror. And I hid behind it, because I had my bad experience, you know. Anyhow.

speaker: interviewee
text: And when they found it, and they raped me. This goes with the war. And next day, again, they started to walk. Then I escaped because I was not so much observed. And then I met a group of children. This was a Red Cross shelter close to Budakeszi. And a few times I took them food to put under the, under the gates. I don't know if they found it or not.

speaker: interviewer
text: So was about 14 children. The oldest was 15 years old. And the youngest was four years old. And they-- I asked him, who are you? They came from this Red Cross shelter and wanted to go to Budapest to find their parents. So I said, I'll go with you. And I thought maybe they will-- I can help them. They were-- Then I went to prefecture. We went to a town. And I told that here is a group of children, and we need some shelter and food. Food they cannot give, but here is an empty house you can go in. So then I told the children, now search the house. We find some food maybe. So really they found somewhere in the cellar some potato. And they were-- I don't know who did that. It was a beautiful pantry, and there were all conserves in it. And every was a hole in it. Somebody put a hole in it.

speaker: interviewee
text: I said, children, this we don't eat. Maybe somebody put poison in it or something. Don't touch it. So we cooked-- the children took apart the whole furniture-- chairs and beds and everything. We made fire, and we cooked the potatoes. And then in the morning when we came out, again we met Russians. And I was a grown up. They took me. They-- I don't know what happened to the children. They let them go maybe. So I was again-- it was the second time.

speaker: interviewer
text: So somehow, again I escaped. I went back to Budakeszi, and I found this family. And there happened terrible things to them. The 16-year-old girl was raped with a group of Russian soldiers. So then I had to stay there for a few days. And then I decided now I am going. And then I-- was no transportation, so I walked. I asked people, how do I get to Budapest? And this was the worst part of it. So we arrived to a town. And I saw Hungarian soldiers. There were already a Hungarian, a Hungarian government in [HUNGARIAN] government. And some soldiers went over to them. And they had the white armband, but they had the weapon. And I told them. In the meantime, I decided now I will be a nurse. In the hospital I got some paper from-- that I worked for them. So somewhere I found a napkin, a damask napkin. I put around my head. And I put somehow-- I don't know where I got-- a red cross. So I was a nurse. And I went to them and asked, who are you? And they said that they are [NON-ENGLISH] soldiers. And they-- and I saw that they have little boats. And they go over the river, because there were no bridges. So I said, listen. I am a nurse, you see? And I can belong to you. Would you take me over, because I have to go to Budapest? I didn't tell them that I am Jewish. So OK. So they took me over to the other side. Then the other side they were Russians, and they took away the weapons. They took away the white armband. And they took them as prisoner.

speaker: interviewer
text: [LAUGHS]

speaker: interviewee
text: And I was between them. So again we walked in this town. And we had to stand in a long line. And suddenly came a Russian soldier and told me this. What are you doing here? I told him.

speaker: interviewer
text: I know you, he said. I saw you to work for us in the hospital in Budakeszi. Are you Jewish? I said, yes. And I told [NON-ENGLISH]. You know what is [NON-ENGLISH]? That, he knows.

speaker: interviewee
text: And he said, listen. They-- you have to get out of here, because these are prisoner of war. And they will take them to Siberia or somewhere to Russia. You have to get out of here. When they go in, they will go in in a yard. Then you don't go where they go. You stay behind, and I will come, and I will take you.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I did what he said. But I didn't wait for him, because I had not very good experience with Russian soldiers, even if he's a Jew. You know? But I was over the river, what was the main thing. And then I walked to Budapest. And I went to my apartment. The whole, the whole street was evacuated, because they were fighting between Buda and Pest.

speaker: interviewee
text: And people were looting-- going in, going gone, bringing things. And I saw that-- you see this vase there. Here a man with this vase in his hand. I had two of them. One already was gone. I got so angry, I started to yell at him. I knew a few words-- what means black [INAUDIBLE], and bad, and the Hungary, and whatever I knew, I told him, yelled him in Russian. And I told prefecture patrol. I pretended that I am a Russian, you know. So he gave me back this thing. But so I went two days to see what is still there. It was only the big furniture what they couldn't take out. Nothing was left. But every day I found something, what somebody took from somewhere. But in my apartment find something better, left that. And one day I come out, and I met on the street Irene. She took my son to a strange place. She was so smart. She didn't go to the town where she used to live. She went to a strange place and said this is my godson. And I took from because of Budapest was bombed. And he told this two and a half years old baby of his false name. And he knew his real name. And he knew his false name, and never, ever mixed up. And he-- she told him that he's not supposed to ask to pee pee if somebody else is there. So then it was a very, very difficult to go to find my son-- was far away and was no transportation. We travelled with Irene on tanks and whatever there were we could find. And so I found my son there. He didn't recognize me. It hurt me. But he had-- full with big wound. The leg-- I saw right away that he has a vitamin C deficiency. So I run around, and I found somewhere a few vitamin C pills. He left-- in the meantime, two men came back-- was hiding somewhere in this town, Ozd. And Irene went to them and told, here is a Jewish child. And I have to go to look for his mother. And this-- they were so happy, because it was not a Jewish woman. Was not a Jewish child around. So my son was like this, because they were bakers. And they fed him with bread, and rolls, and not real food.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were they Jewish?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, they were hiding somewhere-- two Jewish men. There was the whole Jewish community there. And then very, very-- lot of difficulties with interesting and not so interesting part of it. And then we stayed two months there. It were Passover, and there were already 10, about 10 men came back from somewhere. And I was the only Jewish woman and the only Jewish child. And they brought me food. And they baked matzoh. And I made them a Seder. And we all cried, of course. So it was terrible. And then we came back. And in the meantime, somebody moved in in my apartment. I came-- somebody was living in my apartment. He said, I have the right, because I was bombed out. I said, I have more right than you have. And I run around. I always can find some connection. So I got back my apartment.

speaker: interviewer
text: And then these two interesting-- three little interesting things. I-- which one was it first? So I went-- I opened up again my business. And I need that letter we made that time, the [INAUDIBLE] with letter. There was no letter. And somebody told me, listen. Here in a small street is somebody who could hide some letter, and you can maybe get. So I go there, and I told who I am, and I want letter. Are you Mrs. Mautner? Yes. And he brought me this little slide. I said, how did you get to this?

speaker: interviewee
text: I show to you that I am with a child on my arm. And he said, in your apartment, one of my friend who was in this Arrow--

speaker: interviewer
text: Cross

speaker: interviewee
text: --Cross organization, he lived in your apartment. And he found this little slide. And he kept this in his pocket. And he said, I had a feeling that the Virgin Mary and the little Jesus-- Jesus-- and they saved my life. And if ever Mrs. Mautner comes back, give it to him back with a thank-- thanks.

speaker: interviewer
text: One day, I am in the business, and I see that somebody comes in. And I recognize that this was this Mr. [NON-ENGLISH], who was in the sanatorium and who asked to talk to him. And I was so grateful. So I came out, and I say, are you Mr. [NON-ENGLISH]? And he says, yes. I am a long-time customer of yours. We sold wholesale [NON-ENGLISH]. You know? I ordered always by mail. I say, were you in 1944 in the sanatorium in Budakeszi. He says, yes. But how do you know? I ask, do you remember there was there a girl, Irene from [NON-ENGLISH]? I said, but how do you know all of this? I say, because I was this girl. And I want to find out-- you didn't want-- once I talked to you, you never asked me to talk to me again. Why? Why?

speaker: interviewee
text: I thought now he will tell, because I recognize. No. He said, I am a pharmacist. And I have a lot of friends doctors. I didn't want to go to the army. And I got from them a certificate that I have tuberculosis.

speaker: interviewer
text: And they had a friend in this sanatorium. And they kept me there as sick. So I didn't want to talk too much. You know? [LAUGHS] So, again, I was afraid of him. He was afraid of me. This was the second. And the third was, a man came to the business and said, I came to say thank you for you. What for?

speaker: interviewee
text: You saved my life. I was a long-time customer in your store. And you made for me an arch support. And I came that it no good, hurts. Only I wanted to see you. And I was in Ukraine. And was this very cold. And I always saw your face and to tell me, hold on. Hold on. Hold on. And so I feel that you saved my life. Never this man came again. Only came to thank me for his life. And the fourth-- and I think we'll finish with it-- I went out to Budakeszi because I wanted to have from them a certificate that I was working there during the Holocaust time. And I talked to this Mrs. [NON-ENGLISH], the nurse. And she told me that on this day when the Russians came in, the next day she went out, and the dead soldier was lying in the front of the villa, of the pavilion. And since she is a nurse, she went to see if he is still alive and turned him over, because he was lying on his face. And this was Joseph. He wanted to-- maybe to come to me for the clothing. And he was killed in the front. So now I opened again my business in Hungary. They took it away. But luckily I got another one on a better place. And I had to give up my big workshops. I work only with two people. And I became really-- I don't know how I did it. I-- there was no merchandise to work. I organized every, every store where they had some textiles. I needed textiles at the time, where they had. And I found always somebody who put it away and came after and took the bribe. I had everywhere somebody. I don't know how I did that. Today I couldn't do all these things what I could do. Because if it is a must, you can do things. And then the Russian took away my business three times. And three times I got it back. For one, you could bribe everybody.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I went. They made three very beautiful pure silk nightgowns. And I took to someone. I got back my business. Three times this happened.

speaker: interviewee
text: And always I applied that I want to go to Israel. And they didn't let me. Always refuse, refuse. I wrote, I have nobody here, that I have my whole family. I want to go there. Refused. Then came the revolution. You know, in 1956 was a revolution in Hungary. This was a terrible thing. I am sure that you read about that.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I found somebody who, for money, could get me a passport. And they had at that time all the office, everything was open, because there were no government. They were not communists. They're not not communists. Nothing.

speaker: interviewee
text: And he looked up my card. Everybody has a card here, also, not only in the communist country. And it was written that two reasons I was refused. One-- that they needed skilled people. And I had a very good name in my field. The second-- that once they had to give voluntarily, but forced, loyal to the government to build back the bridges and everything. And I gave where I used to live. And then they came to the business. And I have to pay that, also. I said that I paid. That's enough. And they said, no. You have to pay here.

speaker: interviewer
text: I said, I don't pay, because I anyhow don't want to stay in this country. It was on my card. You know? So this was the second reason.

speaker: interviewee
text: There were so terrible things that in Hungary that what we went through. But OK. So after, right after the revolution, I had a friend in the Israeli consulate, so I packed in a pocketbook my jewelry and money-- a few dollars. You know, what we need. And I went to the Israeli consulate. There were standing two Hungarian soldiers. But I put in an open pocketbook on the top my hand kerchief and glasses or what. Because if they catch me, then very bad, you know. They'll put me in jail. And I thought something-- I cannot tell everything, because there were so many things. I was in Prague in jail for a day. So I don't want to go all through these things, because it's unbelievable.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I got a, got a passport, and we could get to Israel. And I see my father after 23 years, and he was dying. And all the doctors-- they didn't know how he kept his life. But he knows that I will come. And I was the first child. And he kept his life. Three days later then we arrived, he died. So I started my life sitting shiva.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I was there in Israel to 1968. And then my son-- in the meantime, he was two years in the army there and finished the first year in the University of Jerusalem. And then he came over to-- he got scholarship here in America. And i came after him. And he already went with a girl. I wanted to see what's going on, so I came to America. And I was here three months, somebody called me in the hotel if I want to come to work. So I was glad. I didn't speak English. I felt lost. I was bored. Was everything so big in New York.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I said, OK, I want to work. And this was German people, so I could communicate. But in the meantime, I learned a few words already. And he reported me to the social security. He should know that I am a visitor, and I have not the right to work. I didn't know.

speaker: interviewee
text: And through the social security, the immigration office found out that I am a visitor only, and I have not the right to work. So they summoned me. They made a whole-- I thought like I would be O.J. Simpson. There were a translator, and a judge, and a prosecutor, and a-- and a-- and a attorney, and somebody from the Jewish Women Organization. And in the meantime, my boss, Mr. Rittenburg ran to the Orthopedic Organization and showed to them that he gave advertisement and couldn't find the help. And we work for sick people, so they cannot leave the hospital without the support, so that they need me.

speaker: interviewer
text: Once maybe I will show to you, somewhere I have a letter that I am indispensable-- how you call it? So the judge saw that and he yelled at me. You have not the right to work! We can deport you right away! But go back and work.

speaker: interviewee
text: [LAUGHS] And it was again-- so I got my green card. And in the meantime, I met Mr.-- my son got married. And I met Mr. Marx. And then I went back to Israel, because in the meantime, I got my green card. And I could leave America.

speaker: interviewer
text: And?

speaker: interviewee
text: And so I was-- went back. I had my business in Israel till 1985. And it was my birthday, and I got a big flower arrangement. And the card, Magda, would you marry me? So.

speaker: interviewer
text: That's a cut.
